summary: Jess Frazelle defines what a distinguished engineer should embody after experiencing the 'N+1 shithead problem' - being demotivated by seeing senior engineers behave poorly. She outlines a comprehensive framework covering technical leadership qualities like empowering others, giving constructive criticism, having opinions loosely held, and prioritizing shipping. The post emphasizes that distinguished engineers must also engage with external communities, remain humble, and ultimately demonstrate these values through actions rather than words.
key_points: Distinguished engineers should empower colleagues to tackle challenges beyond their comfort zone
Having 'opinions loosely held' rather than 'strong opinions, loosely held' prevents overpowering junior voices
Technical leaders must be bridges between teams with strong communication skills
Learning from external communities prevents echo chamber effects and Dunning-Kruger-like blind spots
Shipping and decisiveness matter, but not at the cost of burning out teams
Actions speak louder than words - you must actually do these things
money_quotes: The 'N+1 shithead problem' occurs when you are demotivated by seeing people who are a level above you behave poorly, or more bluntly when they behave like a shithead.
Why would I want to be at their level, when once I get there I'll just be one amongst the dipshits.
I personally believe you don't need a high title to take on a hard task, you just need the support and faith that you are capable of handling it.
Making an active effort not to say 'strong opinions, loosely held' because with a power dynamic that could over power the rest of the voices.
If you silo yourself to only learning within your company, you are missing out on a world of experiences and expertise different than yours from the external community.
Most importantly you must actually do these things. Actions speak louder than words.
key_insight: A distinguished engineer's value lies not in technical brilliance alone, but in their ability to empower others, remain humble, and model the behavior that makes junior engineers want to reach that level.
